news_summary: Advancements in nanoscale heat management, groundbreaking insights into biological chaos, and the transformative impact of AI on mathematical and physical discoveries highlight today's innovative strides in science and technology.
news_summary_fr: Les progrès réalisés dans la gestion thermique à l'échelle nanométrique, les découvertes révolutionnaires sur le chaos biologique et l'impact transformateur de l'IA sur les avancées en mathématiques et en physique illustrent les avancées innovantes actuelles dans les domaines de la science et de la technologie.

articles.title: Heat breaks the rules at the nanoscale and scientists used it to their advantage
articles.summary: Scientists used nanoscale gold metamaterials to supercharge heat transfer across tiny gaps, achieving up to four times more energy flow than similar conventional systems. The breakthrough could lead to better chip cooling, more efficient energy technologies, and a new era of precision heat engineering.
articles.content:
Heat is something we encounter every day. A steaming cup of coffee gradually cools, a laptop warms up during use, and sunlight heats the Earth's surface. Yet when heat is examined at distances far smaller than the width of a human hair, it can behave in unexpected ways.

Researchers from Carnegie Mellon University, working with collaborators at Stanford University and Purdue University, have now demonstrated a powerful new method for controlling heat at the nanoscale. Their findings, published in Nature, provide strong experimental evidence that heat transfer can be intentionally engineered and significantly enhanced using specially designed metamaterials.

How Heat Moves Across Tiny Gaps

The research centers on a phenomenon known as near-field radiative heat transfer. When two objects are separated by an extremely small distance, only a few hundred nanometers, heat can travel between them much more efficiently than it does under ordinary conditions.

Instead of simply radiating outward, thermal energy can effectively tunnel across the narrow gap through electromagnetic waves. This process allows far more heat to flow from one object to another than would normally be expected.

Scientists have understood this effect for years, but experimentally demonstrating how to dramatically strengthen it has remained a challenge.

Metamaterials Boost Heat Transfer

To accomplish this, the researchers turned to metamaterials, engineered materials that contain microscopic repeating structures designed to interact with energy in highly controlled ways.

"Unlike conventional materials, metamaterials are built with tiny, repeating patterns that interact with energy in precise ways," said Sheng Shen, a professor of mechanical engineering at Carnegie Mellon University and senior author of the study. "We patterned microscopic gold structures onto thin membranes and positioned them face-to-face across a nanoscale gap. This increased heat transfer by as much as four times compared to similar setups without metamaterials which is far beyond what traditional physics would predict at larger distances."

The team's experiments showed that the gold-patterned structures substantially increased the amount of heat moving across the gap, achieving heat transfer rates up to four times greater than comparable systems lacking the engineered patterns.

The Science Behind the Effect

The enhancement is not simply the result of adding more routes for heat to travel.

"Rather than simply adding more pathways for heat, the gold structures interact with naturally occurring energy waves in the material, known as surface phonon polaritons, creating a resonance effect," said Zexiao Wang, a PhD student in Professor Shen's research group and co-first author of the study. "These coupled vibrations allow energy to move more freely and efficiently across the gap."

According to the researchers, the effect emerges because the microscopic structures and the material's natural energy waves work together.

"It's a cooperative effect," Shen said. "The structures and the material amplify each other."

Potential Applications in Electronics and Energy

The discovery could have important practical uses. As electronic devices continue to become smaller and more powerful, removing excess heat has become one of the most significant engineering challenges.

Being able to direct and control heat more effectively could lead to improved cooling methods for computer chips and other high-performance electronic systems.

The findings may also benefit energy technologies. Systems known as thermophotovoltaics generate electricity from heat by converting thermal radiation into usable power. Increasing the efficiency of thermal radiation transfer could help make these technologies more viable.

In addition, applications involving infrared sensing could benefit from stronger and more precisely controlled thermal signals. Potential uses range from environmental monitoring to national security.

A Step Toward Engineering Heat

Although the experiments were performed under carefully controlled laboratory conditions and remain limited to nanoscale systems, the work represents an important advance from theoretical predictions to real-world demonstration.

"If heat can be engineered with the same precision as electricity or light, it may open the door to a new class of technologies built not just to withstand heat, but to harness it," Shen said.

This work is supported by the Defense Threat Reduction Agency, the National Science Foundation, and the Air Force Office of Scientific Research. Sheng Shen and Shanhui Fan are the corresponding authors. Zexiao Wang, Renwen Yu, and Hakan Salihoglu contributed equally to this work.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in nanoscale heat transfer using engineered metamaterials, demonstrating experimentally enhanced heat flow beyond traditional limits. This advancement has broad potential applications in electronics cooling, energy technologies, and sensing, offering tangible benefits to society by addressing critical challenges in device performance and energy efficiency. The article provides detailed context and focuses on a single, impactful topic.
articles.personality_title: Scientists boost heat flow using tiny gold structures at the nanoscale
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Heat usually moves from one object to another in ways we see every day, like a warm cup cooling down. But at distances much smaller than a human hair, heat behaves differently. Scientists have been studying a special kind of heat transfer called near-field radiative heat transfer, where heat can jump across tiny gaps much more efficiently than usual.

**What happened** – Researchers from Carnegie Mellon University and partner schools created tiny gold patterns on thin membranes and placed them very close together, just a few hundred nanometers apart. This setup used special engineered materials called metamaterials to increase heat flow across the gap. Their experiments showed heat transfer rates up to four times higher than in similar systems without these patterns.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it shows heat can be controlled and enhanced in new ways at the smallest scales. It could help cool down computer chips better, which is a big challenge as electronics get smaller and faster. It might also improve energy devices that turn heat into electricity and help in technologies that detect infrared signals for things like environmental monitoring.

**What's next step** – The researchers will likely explore how to apply this heat control in real devices and systems outside the lab. As they develop these ideas further, we could see new technologies that use heat more precisely, not just to manage it but to make it useful.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists used tiny gold patterns to boost heat flow across nanoscale gaps, opening new ways to control heat for better electronics and energy devices.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques augmentent le transfert de chaleur grâce à de minuscules structures en or à l’échelle nanométrique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La chaleur se déplace habituellement d’un objet à un autre de manière familière, comme une tasse chaude qui refroidit. Mais à des distances bien plus petites qu’un cheveu humain, la chaleur se comporte différemment. Des chercheurs étudient un type spécial de transfert de chaleur appelé transfert radiatif à champ proche, où la chaleur peut traverser des espaces minuscules beaucoup plus efficacement.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université Carnegie Mellon et d’autres universités ont créé de minuscules motifs en or sur des membranes fines placées très proches, à quelques centaines de nanomètres. Ils ont utilisé des matériaux appelés métamatériaux pour augmenter le transfert de chaleur à travers cet espace. Leurs expériences ont montré un transfert de chaleur jusqu’à quatre fois plus élevé que dans des systèmes similaires sans ces motifs.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle montre que la chaleur peut être contrôlée et améliorée à très petite échelle. Cela pourrait aider à mieux refroidir les puces d’ordinateur, un défi majeur avec des appareils toujours plus petits et puissants. Cela pourrait aussi améliorer les dispositifs qui transforment la chaleur en électricité et aider les technologies de détection infrarouge, par exemple pour surveiller l’environnement.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont probablement chercher à appliquer ce contrôle de la chaleur dans des appareils réels hors laboratoire. En développant ces idées, de nouvelles technologies pourraient utiliser la chaleur de manière plus précise, non seulement pour la gérer mais aussi pour l’exploiter.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des scientifiques ont utilisé de minuscules motifs en or pour augmenter le transfert de chaleur à l’échelle nanométrique, ouvrant la voie à un meilleur contrôle de la chaleur pour l’électronique et les dispositifs énergétiques.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos aumentan el flujo de calor usando diminutas estructuras de oro a escala nanométrica
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El calor normalmente se mueve de un objeto a otro de formas que conocemos, como una taza caliente que se enfría. Pero a distancias mucho más pequeñas que un cabello humano, el calor se comporta de manera diferente. Los científicos estudian un tipo especial de transferencia de calor llamado transferencia radiativa de campo cercano, donde el calor puede saltar a través de espacios muy pequeños mucho más eficientemente.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad Carnegie Mellon y otras universidades crearon patrones diminutos de oro en membranas delgadas y los colocaron muy cerca, a unos pocos cientos de nanómetros. Usaron materiales especiales llamados metamateriales para aumentar el flujo de calor a través de ese espacio. Sus experimentos mostraron tasas de transferencia de calor hasta cuatro veces mayores que en sistemas similares sin estos patrones.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque muestra que el calor puede ser controlado y mejorado de nuevas maneras a escalas muy pequeñas. Podría ayudar a enfriar mejor los chips de computadora, un gran desafío a medida que la electrónica se hace más pequeña y rápida. También podría mejorar dispositivos que convierten calor en electricidad y ayudar en tecnologías que detectan señales infrarrojas para monitoreo ambiental.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores probablemente explorarán cómo aplicar este control del calor en dispositivos y sistemas reales fuera del laboratorio. A medida que desarrollen estas ideas, podríamos ver nuevas tecnologías que usan el calor de forma más precisa, no solo para manejarlo sino para aprovecharlo.

**Resumen en una frase** – Científicos usaron patrones diminutos de oro para aumentar el flujo de calor a través de espacios nanométricos, abriendo nuevas formas de controlar el calor para mejorar la electrónica y los dispositivos energéticos.
articles.image_prompt: Two intricately patterned golden lattices facing each other across a narrow glowing gap, with vibrant waves of warm energy visibly flowing and resonating between them, set against a soft, neutral background in a detailed, warm-toned painting style.

articles.title: Fifty years since a simple equation described the chaos of biology
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 08 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01584-8An exploration of chaos theory in population dynamics showed that unpredictable systems can often be modelled using surprisingly simple mathematics.
articles.content:
Few scientific papers can be said to have inspired works of literature. ‘Simple mathematical models with very complicated dynamics’1 by Robert May, published in Nature in 1976, is an exception. May was a scientific adviser for Tom Stoppard’s 1993 play Arcadia2, which was inspired by chaos theory. This mathematical framework describes systems in which behaviour is incredibly sensitive to the initial conditions and in which evolution can seem unpredictable, even random, although it is in fact governed by well-defined laws. May’s paper, which focused on ecological systems, was foundational to the development of chaos theory and demonstrated one of its key tenets: that complex behaviour can be described using simple mathematical models.

Nature 654, 335-336 (2026)

doi: https://doi.org/10.1038/d41586-026-01584-8

References May, R. M. Nature 261, 459–467 (1976). Stoppard, T. Arcadia (Faber & Faber, 1993). Thorne, K. S. & Blandford, R. D. Modern Classical Physics (Princeton Univ. Press, 2017). May, R. M. in It Must Be Beautiful (ed. Farmelo, G.) 28–45 (Granta, 2003). Letellier, C. et al. Chaos 31, 053110 (2021). Poincaré, H. Acta Mathematica 13, 1–270 (1890). Cartwright, M. L. & Littlewood, J. E. J. Lond. Math. Soc. s1-20, 180–189 (1945). Mayer-Kress, G. & Haken, H. J. Stat. Phys. 26, 149–171 (1981). Poon, C. S. & Barahona, M. Proc. Natl Acad. Sci. USA 98, 7107–7112 (2001). Erguler, K. & Stumpf, M. P. H. Math. Biosci. 216, 90–99 (2008). Nazish, M., Javid, M. & Banday, M. T. Cybersecurity 8, 24 (2025). Gleick, J. Chaos: Making A New Science (Viking, 1987). May, R. M. Science 303, 790–793 (2004). Haldane, A. G. & May, R. M. Nature 469, 351–355 (2011). Download references

Competing Interests M.P.H.S. is a co-founder, director, shareholder and chief scientific officer of Cell Bauhaus PTY Ltd, and is a member of the Australian Academy of Science National Committee for Data in Science.

Related Articles

Subjects
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the 50-year impact of a foundational scientific paper that introduced chaos theory to biology, demonstrating how simple mathematical models can describe complex, unpredictable systems. This breakthrough has broad significance in science and technology, influencing multiple fields and improving understanding of complex natural phenomena.
articles.personality_title: Fifty years of chaos theory transforming our understanding of nature
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In 1976, scientist Robert May published a paper showing that complex and unpredictable behaviors in nature, like how populations grow or shrink, can be explained using simple math. This idea became known as chaos theory.

**What happened** – May’s work revealed that even systems that seem random actually follow clear rules, but tiny changes at the start can lead to very different outcomes. His paper influenced not only science but also art, inspiring a 1993 play called Arcadia that explores these ideas.

**Impact** – This discovery changed how scientists study natural systems. It showed that complicated patterns in biology, weather, and other areas could be predicted better with simple equations. It also helped develop new tools across many fields, from ecology to physics.

**What’s next step** – Researchers continue to use chaos theory to solve problems in areas like population health, cybersecurity, and environmental science. The approach helps improve models that guide decisions about nature and technology.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Robert May’s 50-year-old idea that simple math can explain complex natural chaos still shapes science and helps us understand the world better today.
articles.personality_title_fr: Cinquante ans de théorie du chaos qui transforment notre compréhension de la nature
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – En 1976, le scientifique Robert May a publié un article montrant que des comportements complexes et imprévisibles dans la nature, comme la croissance des populations, peuvent être expliqués par des mathématiques simples. Cette idée est devenue la théorie du chaos.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le travail de May a révélé que même les systèmes apparemment aléatoires suivent des règles claires, mais de petits changements au départ peuvent entraîner des résultats très différents. Son article a influencé non seulement la science, mais aussi l’art, inspirant une pièce de théâtre en 1993 appelée Arcadia qui explore ces idées.

**Impact** – Cette découverte a changé la manière dont les scientifiques étudient les systèmes naturels. Elle a montré que des motifs compliqués en biologie, météo et autres domaines pouvaient être mieux prévus avec des équations simples. Elle a aussi aidé au développement de nouveaux outils dans de nombreux domaines, de l’écologie à la physique.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs continuent d’utiliser la théorie du chaos pour résoudre des problèmes dans des domaines comme la santé des populations, la cybersécurité et les sciences de l’environnement. Cette approche aide à améliorer les modèles qui guident les décisions sur la nature et la technologie.

**Résumé en une phrase** – L’idée vieille de 50 ans de Robert May que des mathématiques simples peuvent expliquer le chaos naturel complexe façonne encore la science et améliore notre compréhension du monde aujourd’hui.
articles.personality_title_es: Cincuenta años de la teoría del caos que transforman nuestra comprensión de la naturaleza
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En 1976, el científico Robert May publicó un artículo que mostraba que comportamientos complejos e impredecibles en la naturaleza, como el crecimiento de poblaciones, pueden explicarse con matemáticas simples. Esta idea se conoce como teoría del caos.

**Qué pasó** – El trabajo de May reveló que incluso sistemas que parecen aleatorios siguen reglas claras, pero pequeños cambios al inicio pueden llevar a resultados muy diferentes. Su artículo influyó no solo en la ciencia, sino también en el arte, inspirando una obra llamada Arcadia en 1993 que explora estas ideas.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento cambió cómo los científicos estudian los sistemas naturales. Mostró que patrones complicados en biología, clima y otros campos pueden predecirse mejor con ecuaciones simples. También ayudó a desarrollar nuevas herramientas en muchas áreas, desde ecología hasta física.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores siguen usando la teoría del caos para resolver problemas en áreas como salud poblacional, ciberseguridad y ciencias ambientales. Este enfoque ayuda a mejorar modelos que guían decisiones sobre la naturaleza y la tecnología.

**Conclusión en una frase** – La idea de Robert May, de hace 50 años, de que las matemáticas simples pueden explicar el caos natural complejo, sigue influyendo en la ciencia y nos ayuda a entender mejor el mundo hoy.
articles.image_prompt: An intricate, warm-toned painting of a delicate, branching mathematical fractal pattern morphing seamlessly into a vibrant, flourishing ecological landscape, symbolizing the connection between simple equations and complex biological chaos, with subtle swirling shapes that evoke dynamic unpredictability under soft natural lighting.

articles.title: How AI is reshaping discovery in maths and physics
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 08 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01820-1Artificial intelligence is not replacing human intuition in these fields, but reimagining how questions are asked, explored and understood.
articles.content: Nature, Published online: 08 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01820-1Artificial intelligence is not replacing human intuition in these fields, but reimagining how questions are asked, explored and understood.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights how AI is positively transforming the process of discovery in mathematics and physics by enhancing human intuition and reshaping inquiry methods, which has broad significance for scientific progress and society.
articles.personality_title: AI is changing how we explore math and physics
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists in math and physics have long used human intuition and traditional methods to understand complex problems. These fields often rely on asking the right questions and exploring patterns to make discoveries.

**What happened** – Recently, researchers have started using artificial intelligence (AI) to help with these discoveries. Instead of replacing human thinking, AI tools are helping scientists ask new types of questions and find patterns that were hard to see before. This change is happening now and is growing quickly.

**Impact** – This is important because AI helps scientists explore ideas faster and in new ways. It can suggest approaches that humans might not think of, making research more creative and efficient. This means discoveries in math and physics could happen more often and lead to better understanding of the world.

**What’s next step** – Scientists plan to continue improving AI tools to work closely with human intuition. They will use AI to explore even more complex problems and develop new theories. Over time, this partnership could change how science is done and lead to breakthroughs in many areas.

**One-sentence takeaway** – AI is helping scientists rethink how they ask questions and explore ideas, making discoveries in math and physics faster and more creative.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’IA transforme notre façon d’explorer les mathématiques et la physique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques en mathématiques et en physique se sont longtemps appuyés sur l’intuition humaine et des méthodes traditionnelles pour comprendre des problèmes complexes. Ces domaines reposent souvent sur la formulation des bonnes questions et l’exploration des modèles pour faire des découvertes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Récemment, les chercheurs ont commencé à utiliser l’intelligence artificielle (IA) pour aider dans ces découvertes. Plutôt que de remplacer la pensée humaine, les outils d’IA aident les scientifiques à poser de nouveaux types de questions et à repérer des modèles difficiles à voir auparavant. Ce changement est en cours et se développe rapidement.

**Impact** – C’est important parce que l’IA aide les scientifiques à explorer les idées plus rapidement et de nouvelles façons. Elle peut suggérer des approches auxquelles les humains ne penseraient pas, rendant la recherche plus créative et efficace. Cela signifie que les découvertes en mathématiques et en physique pourraient arriver plus souvent et améliorer notre compréhension du monde.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques prévoient d’améliorer encore les outils d’IA pour travailler étroitement avec l’intuition humaine. Ils utiliseront l’IA pour étudier des problèmes encore plus complexes et développer de nouvelles théories. Avec le temps, ce partenariat pourrait changer la façon dont la science est faite et conduire à des avancées dans de nombreux domaines.

**Résumé en une phrase** – L’IA aide les scientifiques à repenser la manière de poser des questions et d’explorer les idées, rendant les découvertes en mathématiques et en physique plus rapides et plus créatives.
articles.personality_title_es: La IA cambia cómo exploramos las matemáticas y la física
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos en matemáticas y física han usado durante mucho tiempo la intuición humana y métodos tradicionales para entender problemas complejos. Estos campos dependen de hacer las preguntas correctas y explorar patrones para descubrir cosas nuevas.

**Qué pasó** – Recientemente, los investigadores comenzaron a usar inteligencia artificial (IA) para ayudar en estos descubrimientos. En lugar de reemplazar el pensamiento humano, las herramientas de IA ayudan a los científicos a hacer nuevos tipos de preguntas y encontrar patrones difíciles de ver antes. Este cambio está ocurriendo ahora y crece rápidamente.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque la IA ayuda a los científicos a explorar ideas más rápido y de nuevas maneras. Puede sugerir enfoques que los humanos no imaginarían, haciendo la investigación más creativa y eficiente. Esto significa que los descubrimientos en matemáticas y física podrían ocurrir más seguido y mejorar nuestra comprensión del mundo.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos planean seguir mejorando las herramientas de IA para trabajar junto con la intuición humana. Usarán la IA para explorar problemas aún más complejos y desarrollar nuevas teorías. Con el tiempo, esta colaboración podría cambiar cómo se hace la ciencia y llevar a grandes avances en muchas áreas.

**Resumen en una frase** – La IA está ayudando a los científicos a replantear cómo hacen preguntas y exploran ideas, haciendo que los descubrimientos en matemáticas y física sean más rápidos y creativos.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of an open ancient book with glowing mathematical symbols and physics formulas rising like gentle light beams that intertwine with a softly glowing, abstract neural network pattern, set against a calm, natural background of soft earth tones and subtle gradients, symbolizing the harmonious fusion of human intuition and artificial intelligence in scientific discovery.
